**Overview**  
	The work we have done in previous weeks was reconstruction on a single-image to test our setup as a “proof of concept”. Now, we have shifted away from this single-image framework and towards a more realistic setup. To achieve the results below, the same UPENN-GBM collection was used, but we evaluated on many training slices rather than just one.  
	Our optimization equation largely remained the same, with the addition of a TV regularizer: [![][image1]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=min_%5Calpha%20%7C%7C%20A\(K%5Calpha\)%20-%20y_%7Bobs%7D%7C%7C%5E2%20%2B%20%5Clambda%7C%7CK%5Calpha%7C%7C%5E2#0), where A=MF, which is the undersampling mask, [![][image2]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=M#0), multiplied by the Fourier transform k-space data.  
**Kernel Hyperparameters on Multiple Slices**  
	The first step was multi-slice hyperparameter tuning. Previously, we would find the best kernel settings for one slice, and now they are learned hyperparameters across the dataset. The specific hyperparameters we wanted to learn were kernel width, number of kernels, and TV weight.   
	To do this, we choose candidate hyperparameters, reconstructed a set of training slices, and then computed reconstruction metrics (specifically, MSE and PSNR) for each slice. We averaged those metrics across the slices and selected the hyperparameters that performed the best on the test slices.   
	While the type of kernel was not learned, LaPlacian and Gaussian kernels were used in the single-slice reconstruction and are commonly used across literature. Thus, we just evaluated both types for simplicity. The best hyperparameter values, test MSE and PSNR, and a sample reconstructed slice can be seen for both types below.

| Kernel Type | Sigma | Number of Kernels | TV Weight | Test MSE | Test PSNR |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| LaPlacian | 4.1017 | 1600 | 1.3145e-03 | 0.009615 | 20.173 dB |
| Gaussian | 2.5 | 3844 | 0.0 | 0.007886 | 21.032 dB |

LaPlacian  
![][image3]  
Gaussian  
![][image4]  
	Once the best hyperparameters were found, they were applied to all kernel reconstructions in the rest of the results. Since the Gaussian kernel achieved a lower MSE and higher PSNR than the LaPlacian kernel, the Gaussian kernel was chosen as the kernel type.   
	With the updated kernel hyperparameters, we compared this to the baseline reconstruction of zero-filled FFT for a test slice, which is seen here:  
![][image5]  
As opposed to our single-slice reconstruction, our multi-slice reconstruction returned a much lower PSNR compared to the zero-filled FFT baseline for this particular slice. This almost suggests that applying our next reconstruction techniques to zero-filled FFT would be an optimal area to explore, given that zero-filled FFT is a much simpler method and faster computationally.  
**Residual CNN:**  
	We next used a CNN to act as a refinement step on top of the initial reconstruction method. The basic setup is: [![][image6]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=x_%7Boutput%7D%20%3D%20x_%7Binput%7D%20%2B%20f\(x_%7Binput%7D\)#0), where [![][image7]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=x_%7Binput%7D#0) is either the kernel reconstruction or the zero-filled reconstruction and [![][image8]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=f#0) is the learned residual correction. Essentially, the network doesn’t build the entire image from scratch. It cannot change the measured k-space from the original image. It can only refine the improvements the kernel already made.   
	We performed this setup on both the training set of kernel reconstructed images and on the zero-filled FFT correction for further comparison and to see if there were improvements over the baseline. The evaluation metrics used were MSE, PSNR, and SSIM, which is the structural similarity index. A comparison of the results can be seen here:

|  | Test MSE | Test PSNR | Test SSIM |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Kernel Reconstruction & Residual CNN | 0.002479 | 26.06 dB | 0.4153 |
| Zero-filled FFT Reconstruction & Residual CNN | 0.000478 | 33.20 dB | 0.6758 |

Kernel Reconstruction & Residual CNN  
![][image9]  
Zero-filled FFT Reconstruction & Residual CNN  
![][image10]  
In both cases, the residual CNN improved the reconstruction over the kernel method on its own. However, zero-filled FFT reconstruction continues to outperform the kernel reconstruction, now showing the kernel reconstruction and the residual CNN do not outperform the baseline. This again suggests that exploring reconstructing images from a zero-filled FFT baseline, rather than a kernel baseline reconstruction, could lead to more accurate and faster results.  
**Diffusion:**  
	We next moved towards a diffusion model because of its widespread use in image reconstruction. Our setup was very similar to the residual CNN in the sense that we did not use the diffusion model to reconstruct the entire image, but instead as a way to correct either the kernel reconstruction or the zero-filled FFT reconstruction. First, we conditioned the diffusion model on the initial reconstruction (kernel or zero-filled FFT), then it iteratively corrected the areas of the image that were not measured k-space data (i.e., “real” parts of the image).   
	We again performed the diffusion model correction on the kernel reconstruction and the zero-filled FFT correction as a comparison. The evaluation metrics were MSE, PSNR, and SSIM and the results can be seen here as well as a test slice for visual comparison:

|  | Test MSE | Test PSNR | Test SSIM |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Kernel Reconstruction & Diffusion Model Correction | 0.000690 | 31.61 dB | 0.7796 |
| Zero-filled FFT Reconstruction & Diffusion Model Correction | 0.000772 | 31.12 dB | 0.7620 |

Kernel Reconstruction & Diffusion Model Correction  
![][image11]

Zero-Filled FFT Reconstruction & Diffusion Model Correction  
![][image12]  
	As you can see at this stage, the MSE was lower and PSNR and SSIM were higher for the kernel reconstruction compared to the zero-filled FFT, showing that the combination of the kernel reconstruction and diffusion model actually does better than the baseline approach.

**Literature review on Multiframe Super Resolution Methods:**  
Following the exploration of SRCNNs, we also wanted to explore MFCNNs which generalize single image CNNs to handle multiple frames. To retain spatial information, multiple adjacent frames are concatenated along the channel dimension. This allows the network to use extra pixel information from the surrounding sequence to make a more accurate prediction for the middle frames. We hypothesize that extending these techniques to MRI frame slices will make the image reconstruction stronger by utilizing the contextual patterns that are linked between frames.   
These models use Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), such as Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs), to capture temporal variations across frames.

* **Multi-Level Percepts and the GRU-RCN (Ballas et al., ICLR 2016\)**  
  Earlier CNN layers preserve higher spatial resolution and understand the image at a low level while later layers better identify structures but don’t preserve the low level understanding. Standard approaches only used the final CNN layer. The GRU-RCN instead applies recurrent units at multiple convolutional levels simultaneously and extracts temporal patterns at different spatial scales. Since finer structural details in MRI live in low-level feature maps, this multi-level approach could preserve them better than top layer only methods.  
* **Bidirectional GRU-RCN:** This method processes the sequence in both forward and reverse order, and then concatenates the results. Bidirectionality allows a slice to be informed by both deeper and shallower neighbors simultaneously.  
* **MFSR-GAN with Handheld Motion Modeling (Khan et al., CVPRW 2025):** This paper tackles the issue of when frames aren't perfectly aligned, in particular, the case of hand held video. They used a synthetic data engine that uses real handheld motion (modeled via homography matrices extracted from actual captures) rather than assuming uniform random motion. Second, the MFSR-GAN architecture uses a base frame emphasis strategy, where all other frames are aligned relative to a designated reference frame, explicitly computing feature differences  to highlight misalignments. This suppresses ghosting artifacts that arise when naive frame averaging blurs regions where things have moved. For MRI, we could use these methods to account for movement from patient breathing, heartbeat, or slight movement between scans that introduce misalignments. This approach could help anchor reconstruction around the cleanest or most representative slice.  
* **Attention Mechanisms:** The MFSR-GAN paper also uses channel attention across the fused multi-frame features. After aligning frames, it concatenates them and applies attention to let the network weigh which frames (and which feature channels within those frames) are most informative. This is useful when some frames are noisier or contain motion artifacts and the network learns to downweight them rather than treating all input frames equally


**Self Critique:**

* The diffusion models with data consistency applied performed very well compared to their kernel/zero-filled FFT counterparts. Using zero-filled FFT lowered runtime and memory usage though slightly lowering the structural similarity and PSNR. The long runtime makes parameter tuning difficult, making self-tuning networks a helpful option. We are exploring autoresearch for lowering the noise floor and catching more fine details of the image.  
* Using MRI images throughout our project has allowed us to compare image reconstruction methods week-to-week, but with higher levels of accuracy, it’s difficult to assess model performance. Some fine details on an MRI may be important for clinicians, but we lack that context, questioning the utility of our high-accuracy images.
